Is this a local folder?

Whether is is or not, I would create a new local folder and use the add-on "ImportExportTools NG" to import the old folder file into the newly-created folder. The MSF file is not needed - it will be recreated as needed.

Thunderbird itself will never block sending to an email address... but your email provider might. Please quote any error messages (completely and exactly) you see when you try.

Similarly, Thunderbird won't block incoming email, though it might mistakenly classify it as spam. Again, please describe what happens to this incoming email that makes you consider it blocked.

I do notice that the garmin thing you mention above is not a correctly-formed email address, so it couldn't go anywhere.

content:
This is a server error message, NOT a Thunderbird message. It appears to me that the message is currently held on acsalaska.net's server. It seems to be having trouble delivering it to domain inreach.garmin.com.cdn.cloudflare.net . The message you received says that they will keep trying to deliver it for 5 days before they give up.

The email header analyzer I used mentions that the message failed Sender Policy Framework (SPF) Authentication, which is a security protocol servers use to approve communication between them. I don't know that that's always accurate, and I believe that the failure is not always fatal - it depends on how the servers are set up - but that might account for your message delay. It's a mail server problem and there's nothing you can do about it on your end unless you can reach a tech support person who can control the server or knows who can.

This is pushing my knowledge of how email systems work, but I believe Thunderbird did its work successfully and the message is currently stuck in server limbo.
creator: rednoise

content: Dear Lin: Yes, the problem was the email provider, not Thunderbird. Acsalaska tech support fixed the problem--the messages weren't in the regular spam, but in spam on Alaskacom's webmail. Problem solved, thank you for your help

content:
Is this a POP3 account or IMAP? If IMAP (I don't have a working POP3 account to compare)...

Thunderbird has some settings that can control message retention. See (in Thunderbird for Windows) <Tools | Account Settings | Synchronization & Storage | Disk Space>. If you have it to, Thunderbird may automatically delete messages older than x number of days. There could also be similar settings set by your email provider, not controllable by Thunderbird.

If you have a backup, it might be possible to recover an older copy of the Sent mailbox. Older versions of Thunderbird are not as flexible in this regard as new ones. Try to recover to a safe location the most recent copy of your entire Thunderbird Profile from your backup and we can take it from there.
creator: rednoise

content:
re :I Updated to Your Latest Thunderbird (Default) Update: And Now I Can't Open Addresses in A-Separate Window....

You need to tell us why this is not possible. Are you using a 'double click' action ? Is this set up:

  • Settings > General
  • scroll to 'REading & display'
  • Open messages in:
  • Select 'A new message window' or 'an existing message window'

This means a double click would enact that option.

OR are you right clicking on selected email and choosing 'Open' and then 'Open message in new window' . What options do you see when you select 'Open' ? Upload an image file eg: jpg showing what you see.

re :So I went Back to Previous Installation:

You cannot simply reinstall a previous version and expect it work. Ever since version 68, https://support.mozilla.org/en-US/kb/dedicated-profile-thunderbird-installation

This is what you can do if you want to use that earlier version

  • Exit Thunderbird.
  • Access profile name folder and delete the 'compatibility.ini' file.
  • Start Thunderbird
  • select the correct profile.

If you need additional steps please ask and they can offered.

Please note if you have auto updates operating then remember to update those settings to select tell you about apdate but let you choose whether to install.

content:
The blackmail message is from Comcast, not from Thunderbird itself. That web mail works isn't relevant because the mail servers see the web mail host server as the originator, not your home IP address.

You can do some checking on your own - google "email blacklist check" and you will find several public checkers. But, wrongly or rightly, only Comcast is able to lift the blacklist. You'll have to contact them.
